perturbation.original_text: All disclosures contemplated hereunder shall be completed within six (6) months from the date of the last party to execute this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: All disclosures contemplated hereunder shall be completed within one (1) month from the date of the last party to execute this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the disclosure period to one month might be unreasonably short, especially for complex technological information, and could be argued as hindering the recipient's ability to fully evaluate the confidential information, potentially violating implied duties of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing - New York Law
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Reducing the disclosure period to one month may prevent the Recipient from properly evaluating the information, which could be seen as a breach of good faith, especially if complex technological information is involved.

Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.justification: The modification from six months to one month for disclosures could contradict the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under New York law, as it may not allow sufficient time for the recipient to properly evaluate complex information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states: 'Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' The Perturbation and Law Explanations support this contradiction by highlighting that a one-month period might be unreasonably short and prevent proper evaluation, thus potentially breaching good faith.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain in effect for a period of five (5) years from the date of the last disclosure hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain in effect for a period of ten (10) years from the date of the last disclosure hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the confidentiality period to ten years may be considered unreasonably long for certain types of information, particularly in rapidly evolving technological fields, and may raise concerns under New York's trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: While New York does not have a specific statute limiting the duration of trade secret protection, courts consider the reasonableness of the duration. A ten-year confidentiality period, especially in a fast-moving tech field, could be challenged as unreasonable and thus unenforceable under the broader principles governing trade secret law.

Privileged communications; electronic communication thereof. No communication privileged under this article shall lose its privileged character for the sole reason that it is communicated by electronic means or because persons necessary for the delivery or facilitation of such electronic communication may have access to the content of the communication.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a five-year confidentiality period, while the modified version extends it to ten years. The Perturbation and Law Explanations suggest this extension could be deemed unreasonable under New York trade secret law, particularly in a fast-paced technological environment. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is empty, the Law Explanation indicates that New York courts consider the 'reasonableness of the duration' of confidentiality agreements, implying that an excessively long period could be unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 2 refers to the Civil Practice Law & Rules but does not directly address the duration of confidentiality agreements or trade secrets. However, the absence of a statutory limit doesn't negate the possibility of a court finding a ten-year period unreasonable based on common law principles regarding trade secrets, as the Law Explanation suggests, thus the modification presents a significant legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that it will not use the Confidential, nor disclose the Confidential Information to others through any act or omission by Recipient, except with the prior written consent of Disclosor.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that it will not use the Confidential, nor disclose the Confidential Information to others through any act or omission by Recipient, except at Recipient's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for prior written consent for disclosure would make the confidentiality obligation unenforceable, conflicting with the fundamental purpose of a non-disclosure agreement and potentially violating trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York's trade secret law requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Giving the Recipient sole discretion to disclose information undermines these efforts, making it difficult to claim trade secret protection was maintained.

Privileged communications; electronic communication thereof. No communication privileged under this article shall lose its privileged character for the sole reason that it is communicated by electronic means or because persons necessary for the delivery or facilitation of such electronic communication may have access to the content of the communication.
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires prior written consent from the Discloser before the Recipient can disclose Confidential Information. The changed text gives the Recipient 'sole discretion' to disclose Confidential Information, effectively removing the Discloser's control and the requirement for their consent. The Law Explanation notes that New York's trade secret law requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, and giving the Recipient sole discretion undermines these efforts. This change creates a legal contradiction because it weakens the confidentiality obligation and could jeopardize trade secret protection, as the Discloser no longer has control over who receives the confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient will return to the Disclosor, upon request, any documents or other tangible materials disclosed by the Disclosor or generated by the Recipient pertaining to the Discussion.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will retain indefinitely, any documents or other tangible materials disclosed by the Disclosor or generated by the Recipient pertaining to the Discussion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the clause to require indefinite retention could conflict with data privacy laws if the documents contain personally identifiable information (PII) and there is no legitimate business reason for retaining them. This could also contradict standard business practices and raise concerns about potential misuse of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Information Security Breach and Notification Act
perturbation.law_explanation: If the retained documents contain PII, indefinite retention without a valid reason could violate the spirit of New York's data breach notification law, which emphasizes reasonable security measures and responsible data handling. Unnecessary retention increases the risk of a data breach.
perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates indefinite retention of documents, which, according to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, could violate data privacy laws if the documents contain PII without a legitimate reason. The provided law snippet, NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov ARTICLE 39-F, relates to data security protections. While the snippet itself doesn't explicitly prohibit indefinite retention, the Law Explanation suggests that such retention increases the risk of a data breach, potentially conflicting with the spirit of the law, which emphasizes reasonable security measures and responsible data handling. Therefore, the indefinite retention mandated in the changed text contradicts responsible data handling principles.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California introduces potential conflicts if the parties are primarily located and conducting business in New York, particularly regarding specific provisions like non-compete clauses or intellectual property rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally choose governing law, courts may not enforce the choice if it violates a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest in the dispute. If the parties' relationship and the confidential information are primarily tied to New York, a California choice-of-law clause could be challenged.

perturbation.justification: The change of governing law from New York to California creates a contradiction. The Law Explanation states that if the parties' relationship and confidential information are primarily tied to New York, a California choice-of-law clause could be challenged, implying a violation of legal principles related to choice of law and its enforceability. While the scraped snippets don't directly address choice of law, the Law Explanation highlights the potential unenforceability of the California clause, suggesting a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be amended, superseded or canceled, and any of the terms hereof may be waived, only by a written instrument signed by the parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be amended, superseded or canceled, and any of the terms hereof may be waived, by either a written instrument or oral agreement between the parties.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing oral modifications contradicts the Statute of Frauds if the original agreement falls within its scope (e.g., agreements not performable within one year). New York law requires certain contracts to be in writing to be enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York's Statute of Frauds requires certain agreements, including those that cannot be performed within one year, to be in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought. Allowing oral modifications for such agreements renders the modification unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability up TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability next SECTION 5-702 Requirements for use of plain language in consumer transactions This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a significant contradiction by allowing oral agreements to modify the contract, whereas the original text mandated written instruments for any amendments, superseding, cancellation, or waiver of terms. New York's Statute of Frauds, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically states in SECTION 5-701 that certain agreements, including those not performable within one year, are void unless in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged. The modification directly contravenes this requirement by permitting oral modifications, thus making the changed agreement potentially unenforceable under New York law.
